cards.front: What is the most common cause of small bowel obstruction?
What is the most common cause of large bowel obstruction?

cards.back: Dilated if: Small bowel >3cm, Large bowel >6cm, Cecum >9cm (perforation risk)
No passage of flatus AND feces — indicates complete obstruction

cards.front: How does vomiting differ in SBO vs LBO?
cards.back: SBO: Early, profuse, bilious. LBO: Late, feculent
Bowel obstructed at two points — high risk of strangulation

cards.front: What X-ray sign suggests sigmoid volvulus?
What is the bird's beak sign?
cards.back: Tapered narrowing on contrast enema in sigmoid volvulus
Continuous pain, fever, tachycardia, localized tenderness, elevated lactate

cards.front: What is the initial management of uncomplicated SBO?
cards.back: Conservative: NPO, IV fluids, NGT (drip and suck) for 48-72 hours

cards.front: When is emergency surgery indicated in obstruction?
cards.back: Strangulation, closed-loop, peritonitis, failed conservative (48-72 hrs)
Only anti-mesenteric border of bowel in sac — can strangulate without obstruction

cards.front: What differentiates mechanical from paralytic ileus?
cards.back: Mechanical: colicky pain, visible peristalsis, high-pitched bowel sounds. Ileus: silent abdomen

cards.front: What is the most useful investigation for obstruction?
cards.back: CT abdomen (shows transition point, cause, complications)

cards.front: What is the step-ladder pattern on X-ray?
cards.back: Multiple air-fluid levels at different heights — seen in small bowel obstruction
